segments.s425-g121: برین گونه is an archaising fused phrase meaning 'in this way' (بر + این, written as برین). تا introduces a temporal clause 'until', and خور برآمد ز کوه is a simple past action: خور (the sun, poetic for خورشید) is the subject, برآمد is the past tense verb 'rose/appeared', and ز + کوه is the prepositional phrase 'from/over the mountain'. Word order is subject–verb–prepositional phrase as typical; the whole segment sets the time-span during which the following action (the speaking) continues.
segments.s425-g122: نیامد here is a negative past-form meaning literally 'did not come' and functions idiomatically as 'did not become' or 'did not grow (to be)'; it precedes the subject زبانش ('his tongue' where -ش is the possessive clitic). ز + گفتن indicates cause or source ('from/because of speaking'), and ستوه is a predicative adjective 'weary/tired'. The full construction = 'his tongue did not become weary from speaking' i.e. 'he did not tire of speaking.'
segments.s425-g17: چنین رزمگاهی — Literally “such a battlefield.” چنین is a demonstrative/adjective meaning “such” placed before the noun رزمگاهی (battlefield). که begins a relative clause (”where/that”) which qualifies this battlefield; غران (roaring) precedes and modifies شیر (lions), producing “two roaring lions.”
segments.s425-g18: به جنگ اندر آیند هر دو دلیر — The verb phrase آیند (they come) is third-person plural (classical/persuasive present) meaning “enter/come.” The prepositions به … اندر together give “into/onto the fight” (به جنگ اندر آیند = “enter into battle”). هر دو دلیر follows the verb as a descriptive tag: “both (of them) brave.”
segments.s425-g19: کنون تا چه پیش آرد اسفندیار — کنون is an adverb “now” at the front. تا چه here forms an interrogative phrase (roughly “what/how”), and پیش آرد is the verbal phrase “bring forth/produce” with آرد as the archaic third‑person singular present/presentive. اسفندیار is the subject; the verb–subject order is common in poetry.
segments.s425-g20: چه بازی کند در دم کارزار — چه بازی کند = “what play/stratagem will (he) do,” with کند as present/optative “does/will do.” بازی here means a ruse/stratagem rather than a literal game. در دم کارزار is an adverbial phrase “in the moment (دم) of the battle (کارزار).”
segments.s425-g21: چو بشنید دستان ز رستم سخن — چو is a temporal conjunction “when.” بشنید is past tense “was heard/were heard” (classical narrative past). دستان here is best read as “tales/stories” (the grammatical subject), ز marks source “of/from,” and رستم سخن = “speech/talk about Rostam.” Together: “When tales about Rostam were heard…”
segments.s425-g22: پراندیشه شد جان مرد کهن — پراندیشه (literally “full of thought/tormented in thought”) is a predicate adjective; شد is the past “became.” جان مرد کهن = “the soul/heart of the old man.” The order places the predicate before the nominal phrase, a common poetic inversion: “the old man's soul became anxious/pondering.”
segments.s425-g23: بدو گفت کای نامور پهلوان — بدو is the dative “to him,” and گفت is the past-tense verb “(he) said.” کای is the vocative particle “O,” followed by نامور پهلوان “renowned champion,” addressing the addressee. The phrase thus is “he said to him, ‘O famed champion…’.”
segments.s425-g24: چه گفتی کزان تیره گشتم روان — چه گفتی = “what did you say” (گفتی is the archaic/poetic second‑person past “you said” used interrogatively). کزان = “from which/whereby,” introducing a result clause; تیره گشتم روان = “my spirit/soul became dark/gloomy,” with گشتم past “I became.” The whole means “What did you say that has made my spirit dark?”
segments.s425-g9: This is a reporting clause: زواره is the agent/name (vocative or nominative) and بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb meaning 'commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of content/purpose ('that...'), هرچ is an indefinite pronoun 'whatever', and گفت here functions nominally as 'what was said' (i.e., the content to be brought). Word order is Subject + (honorific) verb + تا + clause.
segments.s425-g10: بیاورد is a third-person subjunctive/jussive form used after تا to express the commanded action ('that ... be brought' / 'that ... bring'), followed by the object گنجور (a treasure-house/treasury) with the possessor marker او 'his'. از + نهفت is a prepositional phrase 'out of hiding' (نهفت = hidden/concealed). The clause reads as a single commanded result: 'bring/produce his treasure-house out of concealment.'
segments.s425-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. رستم is the subject. سلیح نبردش is a genitive/attributive phrase: سلیح 'weapon/armament' modified by نبردش ('of the fight' with third-person possessive -ش), so 'the weapons/arms of his battle' (i.e., the fighting gear); بدید is a classical past-tense verb 'he saw'. Word order: conjunction + subject + object phrase + verb (typical in older Persian).
segments.s425-g12: This is the main clause(s). سرافشاند is a poetic/periphrastic verb meaning roughly 'he cast/threw (his head) back / he put himself into action' (literally 'scattered/upon the head' but used idiomatically). و is the coordinative 'and/then'. باد here denotes a breath or gust ('wind') that he causes; از جگر is a source phrase 'from the jigar' where جگر is the poetic seat of feeling/courage ('heart'/'guts'); برکشید is a past tense 'he raised/drew up' (i.e., drew up a wind/breath). Together they describe a physical/exclamatory motion: he tossed up his head and drew in a powerful breath from his heart/courage.
segments.s425-g13: چنین گفت is a narrative formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus). کای is a vocative particle 'O!' addressing someone or something. جوشن کارزار is a compound vocative: جوشن 'cuirass/armor' modified by کارزار 'of the battlefield/war' (a genitive relationship). The phrase addresses the armor (or metaphorical protector) directly.
segments.s425-g14: برآسودی is a predicative/adjectival perfect-type form meaning 'at ease/relieved' or 'rested from action' (here 'free from fighting'). از جنگ is a prepositional phrase 'from war/battle'. یک روزگار is a temporal phrase 'for a while / for a period' (literally 'one time/period'). Together they form the address' predicate: 'you have been free from battle for a time.'
segments.s425-g15: کنون 'now' sets the temporal frame. کار پیش آمدت is a verbal-nominal cluster meaning 'the matter/task that has come before you' (پیش آمدت = 'has come upon you', آمدت = 'it occurred to you/has befallen you' with enclitic -ت). سخت is an adjective 'difficult/severe'. باش is an imperative 'be' giving exhortation ('be firm/steadfast'). The clause therefore reads: 'Now that a hard matter has befallen you, be firm.'
segments.s425-g16: به هر جای is a locative phrase 'in/at every place' (به = in/at, هر جای = every place). پیراهن بخت is a genitive phrase 'the garment/shirt of fortune' (a metaphorical epithet), and the final باش is again an imperative 'be'. The line is a blessing/exhortation: 'be, in every place, the garment of fortune' (i.e., be fortunate/protected everywhere).
segments.s425-g25: تو is the subject 'you' (poetic 'thou'); تا introduces a temporal clause meaning 'until'; بر + نشستی literally 'sat on' — here the verb نشستی is past tense 'you sat/mounted'. بزین نبرد ('on the saddle of battle') is the locative phrase placed after the verb for poetic word order (older Persian often postpones such phrases).
segments.s425-g26: نبودی is a past/conditional form meaning 'you would not have been'; مگر here means 'unless' or 'except'; the predicate نیک‌دل رادمرد is a nominal description 'a kind‑hearted, noble (or generous) man' that serves as the exception introduced by مگر.
segments.s425-g27: همیشه is an adverb 'always' placed before the noun for emphasis; دل is the subject 'the heart'; از رنج (a prepositional phrase) + پرداخته uses the past participle پرداخته ('having paid/borne') to form a stative/perfect idea 'having borne/been taxed by suffering.' Together they mean 'the heart, always having endured suffering.'
segments.s425-g28: به فرمان شاهان is an instrumental/agent phrase 'by the command of the kings' (به here rendered 'by/at' in poetic usage), and سرافراخته is an adjective 'head‑raised/proud' predicated of the previously mentioned دل — i.e. 'the heart stands exalted at the kings' command.'
segments.s425-g29: بترسم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I fear' (often used to introduce a fearful wish or apprehension); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'; روزت is 'your day'; سرآید is a jussive/subjunctive verb here meaning 'may come to an end' (سرآید can mean 'come' or 'end' depending on context); همی is an emphatic/modal particle adding immediacy ('indeed/just' or ongoing sense).
segments.s425-g30: گر is the conditional 'if'; اختر is 'star' (used metaphorically for fate/fortune); به خواب اندر آید is a light‑verb phrase 'enters into sleep' where به + خواب = 'into sleep' and اندر آید ('comes into') expresses 'fall into slumber'; همی again gives an emphatic/continuous flavor. The whole clause is a conditional 'if the star falls asleep.'
segments.s425-g31: همی here marks a repeated/future action ('they will'); تخم دستان is a poetic genitive 'seed of the hands' i.e. offspring/lineage (literally 'seed of the household/line'); ز بن 'from the root' is a standard idiom for complete uprooting; برکنند is the verb 'they uproot/remove' in the present subjunctive used to express future/inevitable action.
segments.s425-g32: زن و کودکان را is the definite object 'women and children' marked by را; به خاک is the directional complement 'to the earth/to the ground'; افگنند is the verb 'they cast/throw' (third person plural), completing the coordinated result clause.
segments.s425-g57: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بیند is a third-person singular present/subjunctive form of دیدن used in classical/poetic Persian ('he sees' / 'should he see'); ترا is سنخِ مفعولی ('تو' + object marker را) placed after the verb (object-before-verb word order is common in older Persian). کی کند is an interrogative clause: کی 'who' + کند (subjunctive/epistolary 'would/do'), and شاه بد here yields the sense 'do wrong/act ill' in connection with that interrogative — the whole clause asks rhetorically who would do wrong when the king sees you.
segments.s425-g58: خود 'himself' (emphatic, pointing to the king personally). از marks source or origin ('from' / 'on the part of'), so از شاه کردار بد = 'bad conduct originating from the king'. کی سزد is a rhetorical interrogative: کی here introduces the question and سزد is an impersonal present/subjunctive meaning 'it befits' or 'is fitting'. Together they ask whether it is proper that the king himself show bad conduct.
segments.s425-g59: بدو is the prepositional/adverbial phrase 'to him' (ب + دو) meaning 'unto him'; گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (classical text often writes گفت for 'he said'); رستم is the speaker's name (subject). که‌ای is a vocative/introductory particle (که + ای) addressing the other person — 'O...' — followed by مرد پیر 'old man' as the addressee.
segments.s425-g60: سخنها is the plural object 'words / speeches'. برین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this manner' (بر + این/برین + گونه 'this kind/way'); note بر precedes the demonstrative. آسان مگیر is a negative imperative: آسان 'lightly/easily' and مگیر (م + گیر) is the prohibitive 'do not take' (present-subjunctive/imperative form). The clause therefore issues the command 'do not take the words so easily.'
segments.s425-g61: به مردی is an adverbial phrase 'in/for manhood' or 'as a man' (به + مردی). مرا is the experiencer/dative 'for me' (here split in the word list), and سال بسیار گشت is a verbal phrase where سال بسیار 'many years' is the subject/phrase and گشت (past of گشتن) means 'turned/elapsed / became' — together 'many years have gone by for me.' Word order places the temporal phrase before the verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s425-g62: بد و نیک lists contrasting qualities 'bad and good'. چندی here means a limited span ('for a while' / 'some time'), بسر is a verbal-nominal compound meaning 'to spend/pass (time)' and بر گذشت (بر + گذشت) gives the past action 'passed' or 'was spent.' Together the clause says that both bad and good times have, for a while, passed.
segments.s425-g63: رسیدم is first-person past 'I arrived/reached'. به marks direction/location 'to/at'. دیوان مازندران is a genitival phrase 'the Divs (or court/assemblage) of Mazandaran' — in the epic context دیوان means the supernatural 'divs' (demons), not the poetic 'collection'; that specialized sense is not listed in the provided options. The whole phrase states the arrival at Mazandaran's divs/seat.
segments.s425-g64: به رزم is 'to/for battle' (به + رزم) indicating purpose. سواران is the plural 'horsemen/mounted warriors' and هاماوران names the group/people involved (here a place-name or tribal name); in context it means 'the mounted warriors of Hamavaran'. The phrase as a whole indicates arriving there to engage in battle with those horsemen.
segments.s425-g1: چو is the archaic/poetic conjunction meaning “when.” رستم is the proper name (subject). بیامد is the past-tense verb ‘came’ (bi-āmad, a literary form). به + noun marks destination, so به ایوان خویش = “into/to his iwan”; خویش is expressed as خوی + ش, where ش is the enclitic possessive “his.” Word order: temporal clause (چو + subject + verb) followed by the prepositional destination phrase.
segments.s425-g2: نگه کرد is the compound verb نگه‌ داشتن/کردن used here in past tense meaning “he looked / he gazed.” چندی is an adverbial phrase “for a while.” به دیوان خویش is a prepositional phrase meaning “at/onto his diwan (his court/assembly)”; diwan here denotes the court/royal office rather than a book of poems. The object phrase precedes or follows the verb in classical style; here the verb + duration + prepositional object form a single action.
segments.s425-g3: زواره is a proper name (here a person coming). بیامد is past-tense ‘came.’ به نزدیک is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “near/close to”; the following اوی (an older/pronoun form) marks the person to whom he came — literally “near to him.” Word order: subject + verb + prepositional phrase (destination/position + clitic pronoun).
segments.s425-g4: ورا is the archaic object pronoun meaning “him / to him” (direct object form). دید is the past tense ‘saw / beheld.’ پژمرده و زردروی are predicative/adjectival descriptions of that person — “withered/pale and sallow-faced.” Note classical order: object pronoun (ورا) appears before the verb, and adjectives follow to describe that object.
segments.s425-g5: بدو گفت is a classical word order where بدو (= 'to him/unto him') precedes گفت (‘said’), so literally “to him he said.” The following clause contains an imperative: رو ... بیار. بیار is the present-imperative form of آوردن (“bring”). رو here functions adverbially/imperatively to mean something like “forward” / “bring forth” (common in older/epic usage) and precedes the direct object تیغ هندی ‘the Indian sword.’ Overall: he addresses someone and issues the command to bring the sword.
segments.s425-g6: یکی is the indefinite numeral/quantifier “one / a certain,” here introducing items to be brought. جوشن is a cuirass/armor; و links the next item; مغفری is the name/type of another piece (here used as a piece of equipment) and نامدار is a postpositive adjective meaning “renowned / famed.” Word order: quantifier + noun (+ conjunction + noun) + adjective after the final noun (a common pattern).
segments.s425-g7: This segment is a string of nominal imperatives formed by noun + آر. In epic Persian آر functions like an imperative particle meaning “bring” or “provide” after each item (here repeated for rhetorical listing). کمان is ‘bow.’ و is the conjunction linking the list. برگستوان (an item for the arm/shoulder — arm-guard) follows the same pattern; the sequence shows iterative commands to bring each piece of equipment.
segments.s425-g8: This continues the catalog: کمند آر = “bring the lasso/snare” (کمند = lasso; آر = bring). و links to گرز گران آر where گرز is ‘mace/club’ and گران is the postpositive adjective ‘heavy/weighty’ (adjectives commonly follow nouns in this poetic register). The final و گبر adds another item (گبر here is a noun — in context likely an epithet or a named item/person); overall the line is a rhythmic list of commanded gear, with nouns followed by آر or linked by و.
segments.s425-g113: تو گویی که از باره بردارمش — تو گویی is an address meaning “you say / you would say” (a poetic present), introducing a reported or hypothetical clause with که “that”. از باره literally “from the burden/pack”: از = “from” + باره = “burden/load”. بردارمش is composed of بردار (root ‘to lift/take’) + م (1st‑person ending: I) + ش (object clitic ‘him/it’), so it means “I will/should take/lift him.”
segments.s425-g114: به بر بر سوی خان زال آرمش — به بر is a stock classical phrase meaning “into the arms / to the breast” (به = to/toward, بر = upon/on = ‘bosom/arm’). The repeated بر is a poetic/metrical reinforcement of location (‘into/on the arm’). سوی = “toward,” followed by the addressee/title خان زال “Khan Zāl.” آرمش is a poetic/contractional form here meaning “I will carry/bring him” (likely a metrically shortened form of آورمش), i.e. the speaker’s action of bringing the person to Zāl.
segments.s425-g115: نگوید چنین مردم سالخورد — نگوید here is an archaic/poetic verbal form that can be read as an imperative negative (“do not say”) or as a present/past narration depending on context; syntactically it commands or reports negation. چنین modifies مردم “such/so” and سالخورد is a postpositive adjective meaning “aged” or “venerable,” so the phrase addresses or refers to ‘such aged people.’
segments.s425-g116: به گرد در ناسپاسی مگرد — به گرد در = “around/about the door” (به گرد = around/about, در = door/threshold). ناسپاسی is a noun meaning “ingratitude.” مگرد is an archaic negative/imperative particle here meaning “do not turn / do not become” (literally ‘do not go into’/‘do not be’), so together the clause warns against acting with ingratitude at the door/threshold.
segments.s425-g117: بگفت این و بنهاد سر بر زمین — بگفت is the narrative past “he said” (be‑ prefix + گفت). این = “this” (refers to the preceding words). و links the clauses. بنهاد is an archaic past of نهاد/نهادن meaning “he placed/laid”; it takes سر “head” as its object. بر زمین = “upon the ground” with the preposition بر preceding the locative noun.
segments.s425-g118: همی خواند بر کردگار آفرین — همی is an iterative/adverbial particle that with a verb gives habitual or continuous action in narrative (here: ‘kept on’ / ‘continuously’). خواند is used as ‘he recited/sang’ (a performative verb). بر introduces the indirect object ‘to’ (to the one named), کردگار = “the Creator/God,” and آفرین is the exclamation/praise (‘praise’/‘acclamation’). The whole means he repeatedly offered praise to God.
segments.s425-g119: همی گفت کای داور کردگار — همی گفت again expresses repeated utterance (“he kept saying”). کای is a classical vocative particle meaning “O” (addressing someone). داور = “judge” (here a divine epithet) and کردگار = “the Creator/God”; the phrase is a vocative invocation to God as judge/creator.
segments.s425-g120: بگردان تو از ما بد روزگار — بگردان is an imperative/request meaning “turn away/avert” (here a prayer). تو is the vocative ‘you’ (addressing God). از ما = “from us.” بد روزگار is a classical compound meaning “evil/bad fate” (بد = bad, روزگار = fate/times). Together: a plea that God remove bad fortune from them.
segments.s425-g97: نشانمش is a first‑person verb with the object suffix -ش (I + him/it). Here it functions as 'I will set/place him' (classical narrative uses simple present for a near action). بر introduces the following noun phrase; نامور تخت عاج is the noun phrase 'the renowned (نامور) throne (تخت) of ivory (عاج)', with عاج postmodifying تخت as material, so بر + این گروه = 'upon the renowned ivory throne'.
segments.s425-g98: The verb (here written in the line as نهم, from نهادن) is first‑person 'I place/put'. بر سرش is the prepositional phrase 'on his head' (سر + pronominal suffix -ش = 'his head'). بر دل‌افروز تاج is the direct descriptive object 'the heart‑delighting crown' (دل‌افروز qualifying تاج). Poetic word order repeats بر and places the object after the locative phrase: 'I place, upon his head, the heart‑delighting crown.'
segments.s425-g99: ببندم is the 1st‑person present/jussive form of بستن (here 'to bind/gird'), meaning 'I will gird'. کمر is the body part/girdle ('waist' or 'belt') and together کمر ببندم = 'I gird (my loins)'. پیش او = 'before him / in his presence'. بنده‌وار is an adverbial/qualifying word 'in a servantly/humble manner', modifying the action.
segments.s425-g100: نجویم is the negative present/jussive (ن‑ + جویم) meaning 'I will not seek / I do not wish'. جدایی is the noun 'separation' which functions as the object of نجویم. ز is the poetic preposition 'from', and اسفندیار is the name 'Esfandiar' — together 'not seek separation from Esfandiar.'
segments.s425-g101: تو دانی is present 'you know'. که introduces the subordinate clause 'that'. من پیش تخت قباد places the speaker temporally/locatively 'I before the throne of Qobad' (پیش = 'before / in the presence of'), setting up the clause whose verb follows in the next segment.
segments.s425-g102: چه کردم is the interrogative/indirect question phrase 'what I did' (here functioning as the content of 'you know'). به مردی is an adverbial/instrumental phrase 'in/by manliness' or 'as a deed of manhood' (به + abstract noun مردی). تو داری به یاد uses the light verb داشتن (داری) with به یاد (an idiom 'to have in memory') meaning 'you remember/you keep in mind.' The clause thus says the addressee remembers the speaker's action as a feat of manliness.
segments.s425-g103: بخندید is the past tense 'laughed'. از گفت او = 'at/from his saying' (i.e., 'at his words'). زال زر is the name with an epithet: زال (the hero) followed by زر ('gold') used appositionally as an epithet ('Zal the Golden'). The verb precedes the subject here (poetic order): 'laughed at his words, Zal the Golden.'
segments.s425-g104: زمانی is an adverb 'for a while'. بجنبید (past) = 'moved/stirred' (here intransitive). ز اندیشه = 'from thought' (ز = 'from'), and سر 'head' is the entity that moved; together 'for a while the head stirred from thought' (i.e., his head stirred / he was roused from thought). Word order is poetic (adverb + verb + ablative phrase + subject).
segments.s425-g89: بیارم is a colloquial/poetic contraction of بیاورم, a first-person singular volitional/present-subjunctive meaning “I will bring / let me bring.” نشانم is نشان + the possessive suffix -م (“my”), so the noun appears before the verb in the protasis here (verb + object order is common in Persian poetry). بر is the preposition “on/upon,” تخت is “throne,” and ناز is an attributive noun modifying تخت (a poetic adjective: delicacy/affection/pride).
segments.s425-g90: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial phrase (“after that / thereafter”). گشایم is the first-person present/subjunctive of گشادن (to open) used here with a volitional sense “I will open.” در here refers to the gate/door sense (درِ گنج = the treasure-door), گنج is the treasure or treasure-house, and باز qualifies the door as open/wide (poetically ‘open’ or ‘wide’).
segments.s425-g91: چو here means conditional/temporal “if/when.” مهمان من بوده باشد combines بوده (past participle/compound perfect) with باشد as an auxiliary to give the sense “has been” or “should have been” — a typical Persian periphrastic perfect; روز quantifies the span (“three days”). The clause expresses a hypothetical condition: ‘if he has been my guest three days.’
segments.s425-g92: چهارم is ‘the fourth (day).’ چو here functions as a temporal ‘when.’ از چرخ گیتی فروز is a compact poetic phrase: از + چرخ گیتی = ‘from the sky/heaven of the world,’ and فروز (adjective/verb) = ‘shines / shines forth / is bright,’ describing the rising/appearing on that fourth day.
segments.s425-g93: بیندازد is a third-person present/future (poetic) of انداختن with the prefix بی- (he casts/throws down). آن is the demonstrative ‘that,’ and چادر here is a curtain/veil (a covering). لاژورد is the adjective ‘lapis / ultramarine,’ modifying چادر.
segments.s425-g94: پدید آید is a two-part verbal expression: پدید (appearing) + آید (comes) = ‘appears / comes into view.’ از introduces the source ‘from,’ جام is ‘cup,’ یاقوت is ‘ruby,’ and زرد is the color ‘yellow’ (a poetic image: a yellowish ruby or golden-red gleam).
segments.s425-g95: سبک باز is an adverbial phrase meaning ‘lightly/loosely’ (سبک = light; باز = loose/open) modifying the action. با او = ‘with him / around him,’ and ببندم is the first-person present/subjunctive of بستن (to bind/tie) meaning ‘I will bind/tie.’ کمر is the belt or girdle being fastened.
segments.s425-g96: This clause contains a small syntactic inversion common in poetry. نهم (not listed among the tokens) is the verb نَهَم / نَهَدن (I put/place) meaning ‘I place’; وز here is a connective particle in context (poetic usage) introducing the following act — none of the offered lexical senses fully cover that connective function. ایدر = ‘here,’ سوی = ‘toward,’ گشتاسپ is the personal name (Gushtasp), and سر is ‘head’ (anatomical). Together: ‘and from here I place his head toward Gushtasp.’
segments.s425-g65: A nominal phrase: همان رزم کاموس و خاقان چین = 'that/same battle of Kamus and the Khagan of China.' همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'the same') placed before the head noun رزم. کاموس is a proper name here (the name of the commander/king) joined with خاقان چین by و ('and'); چین is in an attributive position to خاقان (Khagan of China). Word order is demonstrative — noun phrase — coordinated title.
segments.s425-g66: A relative clause introduced by که ('which/that') giving the effect of the battle: لرزان بدی زیر ایشان زمین. لرزان is an adjective/participle meaning 'trembling' and is predicative here; زیر ایشان زمین is the locative phrase 'the earth beneath them.' بدی is a terse, slightly archaic verbal/noun-form used here to express the result/effect (literally 'caused/made' or an action-result), so the clause reads 'which made the earth tremble under them.' Note the order: relative particle → participle/adjective → verb-form → locative phrase.
segments.s425-g67: A protasis with اگر ('if'): اگر من گریزم ز اسفندیار = 'If I flee from Esfandiyar.' گریزم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive (classical Persian form used for a conditional: 'if I were to flee' / 'if I flee'). ز is the poetic preposition 'from.'
segments.s425-g68: An admonition/advice clause addressed to تو: تو در سیستان کاخ و گلشن مدار. در سیستان is a locative phrase 'in Sistan.' کاخ و گلشن is the coordinated object 'palaces and gardens.' مدار here functions as a jussive/admonitive predicate meaning roughly 'do not make/consider (them) your domain/realm' (literally: 'make not ... your مدار'); in classical usage مدار can express the idea of a sphere/realm or be used idiomatically to tell someone not to establish such things as their domain.
segments.s425-g69: Temporal clause introduced by چو ('when'): چو من ببر پوشم به‌روز نبرد = 'When I put on the tiger (skin) on the day of battle.' ببر is the object 'tiger' (here understood 'tiger‑skin'); پوشم is first‑person singular present/subjunctive ('I put on' / 'I shall put on') used inside the temporal clause; به‌روز نبرد is an adverbial phrase 'on the day of battle.'
segments.s425-g70: Main clause: سر هور و ماه اندرآرم به گرد = 'I will place/hang the heads of Hoor and Mah about (my) neck.' The direct object (سر هور و ماه) precedes the verb اندرآرم (first‑person singular 'I place/bring in'), a common classical word order; به گرد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around/about' (here 'around the neck'). Note هور و ماه are poetic names/images (light/moon or proper names) and are coordinated by و.
segments.s425-g71: A causal/introductory phrase: ز خواهش که گفتی بسی رانده‌ام = 'On account of the request that you spoke of, I have driven away many.' ز is the poetic preposition 'because of / from'; که گفتی is a relative clause 'that you said' modifying خواهش; بسی رانده‌ام is a perfect/resultive clause 'I have driven away many' (رانده‌ام is the perfect of راندن).
segments.s425-g72: بدو دفتر کهتری خوانده‌ام = 'To him I have read (or presented/called) a lesser/inferior book.' بدو is the dative 'to him'; دفتر is 'book'; کهتری is an adjectival/noun form meaning 'inferiority/lesser'; خوانده‌ام is perfect first‑person ('I have read'), but خواندن can also mean 'to call/name' in older usage, so the phrase is ambiguous between 'I have read to him an inferior book' and 'I have called him inferior' — the literal surface reading is the former.
segments.s425-g73: همی (an older/perfective-progressive particle) here gives a continuous/future-leaning aspect; خوار together with گیرد forms the light-verb construction خوار گرفتن “to hold (someone) cheap/despise” where خوار is adjectival and گیرد is the present/3rd-singular verb (classical form). سخنهای من is a possessive noun phrase (سخنهای = my/one's words/utterances in plural; من follows as the possessor).
segments.s425-g74: بپیچد is a prefixed present/subjunctive-form (بـ + پیچد) meaning “turn/turn away” in the 3rd-singular; سر is the direct noun “head”; از introduces the source/away-from phrase (here “from”); دانش و رای من is a coordinated genitive: دانش “knowledge” and رای “counsel/opinion/plan” followed by من as the possessor (“my”). Word order is verb-final inside the clause, and the verb بپیچد takes سر... از ... as its object/complement.
segments.s425-g75: گر is the classical conditional particle “if”; او is the 3rd-singular pronoun “he”; سر ز کیوان فرود آردی is a nominal+prepositional phrase + verbal phrase: سر (head) + ز (poetic variant of از, “from/away from”) + کیوان (the star Saturn / astrological figure) + فرود (descent/lowering) + آردی (archaic 3rd-singular present 'he brings/does', classical form of آوردن). The whole phrase means “if he brings/lowers his head from Kiwan.”
segments.s425-g76: روانش is روان + possessive -ش (“his spirit/soul/mind”); بر is the preposition “upon/toward”; من is the 1st-person object “me”; درود آردی is a verb phrase with درود “greeting/blessing” as object and آردی the classical 3rd-singular verb ‘brings/sends’. Together: “his spirit would send/give a greeting to me.” Note the classical verb ending -ی (آردی).
segments.s425-g77: ازو is the elided/enclitic form of از او “from him/of him”; نیستی functions here as a statement of non-existence / lack (classical syntax equivalent to “there is not”); گنج و گوهر are coordinated nouns “treasure and jewel(s)”; دریغ follows to indicate (classically) a notion of scarcity/absence or ‘no cause for regret’ depending on context — syntactically it completes the negative statement about absence/scarcity.
segments.s425-g78: The pairings are joined by repeated نه ... و نه ... “neither ... nor ...” negation. Each noun (برگستوان, گوپال, تیغ) is another item in the list that the preceding clause says is not lacking. The construction is a simple coordinated negative enumeration; word order keeps the negator نه before each item for emphasis.
segments.s425-g79: سخن چند گفتم is an ordinary past-tense clause: سخن “words/speech” modified by چند “a few/several” and followed by گفتم (first-person past “I said”); به چندین نشست is a prepositional temporal/adverbial phrase (به + چندین = ‘in several/many a’ + نشست ‘session/sitting’), giving the occasion where the speaking occurred. Note classical flexibility: the quantifier چند precedes the noun it modifies.
segments.s425-g80: This clause is somewhat inverted/classical: ز گفتار (“from/of speech”) starts the line; باد can be the literal noun “wind” (here used metaphorically) though in classical Persian it can also be an optative particle; است is the copula “is”; ما را is the 1st-person plural with the object/topic marker را (literally “us” marked), and به دست is a locative/instrumental phrase “in (the) hand” = “under control/power.” A literal reading: “From speech (is) wind; (it) is in our hand,” i.e. the effect of speech is like wind and our fate/power is in its hand — note the classical word-order inversion and topicalization with را.
segments.s425-g49: 'به گنج و به رنج' repeats the preposition به before each noun for balance/emphasis ('to/into treasure and to/into hardship'). 'این روان' is a nominal phrase 'this soul/this man' placed before the verb (the verb appears in the next segment — Persian often places objects/phrases before the verb). 'بازخر' here functions as an appositive epithet or name attached to 'این روان' (poetic compound/label), completing the direct-object phrase that the following imperative will act on.
segments.s425-g50: مِبَر (written مبر) is a negative imperative ('do not take/bring'). 'پیش دیبای چینی' is a prepositional phrase 'before/in front of Chinese brocade' (پیش + noun). The direct object تبر ('axe') follows the prepositional phrase for emphasis; the overall clause is a command prohibiting bringing a crude/tool (تبر) into the presence of splendid cloth (دیبا چینی).
segments.s425-g51: سپاه ورا places the noun سپاه ('army, troops') before the dative clitic ورا ('to him/for him'), so 'an army for him'. خلعت آرای is a compact expression meaning 'bestow/clothe with the robe (of honour)' where خلعت is 'robe' and آرای (here used as a nominal/poetic-form) expresses adornment; نیز 'also/too' links this command to the previous line. The phrase reads as an imperative sequence of acts to be done for 'him'.
segments.s425-g52: ازو 'from him' introduces what follows as taken from that person. باز is the adverb 'again.' خر خویشتن را is 'his own donkey' — خویشتن used emphatically as a possessive and را is the definite/object marker attaching to the phrase; به چیز ('for a thing') indicates in exchange or for some trifle. The main verb is implied (imperative context continues), so the sequence means 'again take his donkey from him for some thing.'
segments.s425-g53: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. برگردد is third-person singular present/subjunctive form of برگشتن ('to return/turn back') used in a temporal subordinate clause. او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. از + لب هیرمند = 'from the bank (لب = brink/bank) of the Helmand (هیرمند = Helmand river)'. Word order is typical older Persian: conjunction + verb-form + subject + prepositional phrase.
segments.s425-g54: This is an imperative instruction addressed to تو ('you' — here poetic/thou). پای اندر آور is a verb phrase where پای (foot) is put before the verb آور ('bring/put') — 'put your foot in'. اندر marks inward direction ('into/inside'). به رخش بلند is a prepositional phrase 'onto/into (به) [your] steed (رخش) lofty/high (بلند)'; رخش is 'steed/warhorse'. The phrase means 'mount/put your foot into your tall steed.'
segments.s425-g55: چو ایمن شدی is a temporal clause 'when you become/are safe' (چو = when; شدی = second-person singular perfect/past of شدن used to mean 'you are/you became'). بندگی کن is an imperative 'do servitude' = 'act humbly/serve' (noun + کردن forming an instruction). به راه is a locative/prepositional phrase 'on/in the road/way' indicating where the servitude should be shown.
segments.s425-g56: بدان تا introduces a following purpose/temporal clause. بدین (here written ب + دان) is archaic/compact; in context it functions as 'be/stay there' / 'remain there' (the exact morphological pieces are older). تا marks purpose/limit 'until/so that', and ببینی is the subjunctive/present (you may see) of دیدن. یکی روی شاه = 'one/a certain face of the king' i.e. 'the king's face' or 'the king himself' (یکی = a certain/one; روی = face; شاه = king).
segments.s425-g81: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/poetic particle meaning 'thus/so' or 'in this way' (here adding the sense 'even so' to the condition). فردا is the temporal adverb 'tomorrow' placed before the verb. کند is the third-person singular verb 'makes/does' used here in a subjunctive/hypothetical sense after گر ('if he should do/make'); کارزار is the object 'battle/war' — the verb + object pairing is slightly inverted by the poet (کند کارزار instead of the more colloquial کارزار کند).
segments.s425-g82: دل از جان او forms a coordinated possessive phrase 'his heart and (his) life' with از linking the two nouns in a classical/poetic way (lit. 'heart from life of him' → 'his heart and life'). هیچ is an emphatic negative 'at all/none', and رنجه is the verb-root meaning 'be distressed / suffer' used transitively here (i.e. 'cause to suffer'). مدار is an archaic/poetic prohibitive particle attached to the verb phrase to make a negative command ('do not ...'); the whole segment therefore issues a prohibition: 'do not in any way cause his heart or life to suffer.'
segments.s425-g83: نپیچم is a first-person negative (subjunctive/promise) 'I will not twist/entangle' formed with the negative prefix ن-. به + آورد here marks direction/goal 'into/for the fray' (آورد used in a noun-like, battle-related sense in classical usage), با او 'with him' marks accompaniment, and عنان 'the reins' is the direct object. Word order is poetic: the verb نپیچم comes upfront and the goal 'به آورد' precedes the instrument 'عنان'.
segments.s425-g84: نه ... نه is the correlative 'neither ... nor' construction. بیند is the third-person present/subjunctive of 'to see' used here in a potential sense ('he will see / may see'). گوپال in this line appears to name a striking instrument or opponent (contextually 'club' or similar), but the provided lemma senses are proper-name/cowherd and do not match the weapon sense (so the semantic index is not available). زخم سنان is a genitival phrase 'wound of the spear' (زخم = 'wound', سنان = 'spear'). The whole clause means 'he will see neither the club nor the spear-wound.'
segments.s425-g85: نبندم is the first-person negative/subjunctive of بستن 'to bind/fasten' ('I will not bind'). به آوردگاه is a locative phrase 'to/at the battlefield' (آوردگاه = 'battlefield') and راه اوی is 'his path/way' where اوی is the archaic possessive 'his'. The verb comes before the locative phrase in this poetic order, so 'I will not bind (him) to his path to the battlefield.'
segments.s425-g86: بنیرو is an adverbial phrase 'by force / with might' modifying the following verb. نگیرم is first-person negative/subjunctive of گرفتن 'to seize/take' ('I will not take/seize'). کمرگاه اوی is 'his waist/loin' with اوی marking possession. Together: 'I will not, by force, seize his waist' — i.e. 'I will not forcefully take hold of him.'
segments.s425-g87: ز باره is a poetic prepositional phrase 'because of/for this occasion' (ز = 'because/of' in literary usage, باره = 'occasion/instance'). به آغوش بردارمش contains the verb بردارم 'I will take' with the enclitic object -ش (him) attached (بردارمش = 'I will take him into'), and آغوش 'embrace/arms' is the goal 'into my arms.' The whole phrase: 'For this occasion, I will take him into my arms.'
segments.s425-g88: به شاهی is 'to kingship' or 'to the throne' (شاهی here = 'kingship/kingship-seat'). ز گشتاسپ is a genitive/attributive phrase 'of Gushtasp' (ز used poetically as 'of/from'). بگذارمش is the verb گذاشتن with the object enclitic -ش: 'I will place/put him' (here 'I will set him on the kingship/throne of Gushtasp'). The verb form is first-person with the object suffix, typical of classical Persian poetic diction.
segments.s425-g41: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). او is the subject 'he'. شهریاری ز ایران is the object phrase 'a ruler/king of Iran' with ز as the poetic genitive 'of/from'. بکشت is an archaic past form (classical Epic Persian) corresponding to کشت 'killed' (3rd person singular past); the preverb ب- appears as part of the older verbal form.
segments.s425-g42: بدان کو is a compact relative/purpose expression meaning roughly 'to/for that one who' (here introducing the cause: 'because he...'). سخن گفت is a light-verb construction where سخن 'speech/words' combines with گفت (here functioning as 'said/spoke') to mean 'spoke'. با وی درشت is a prepositional phrase 'to him harshly' with درشت describing the manner ('harsh/rough').
segments.s425-g43: همی باش is a continuous or emphatic imperative 'remain/stay (always)'. در پیش او is the locative phrase 'in his presence/before him'. بر به پای is an archaic posture phrase literally 'on (your) foot(s)' meaning 'on your feet' (بر + به + پای yields the stance indication). Word order reflects older/poetic usage.
segments.s425-g44: وگرنه 'otherwise' begins the conditional warning. هم‌اکنون 'right now' is an adverbial intensifier. بپرداز is an imperative (classical form) instructing action ('deal with/perform' idiomatically here 'give up' or 'remove'), and جای is the direct object 'place/position' — so the clause orders an immediate yielding of one's place.
segments.s425-g45: به بیغوله‌ای شو فرود is an imperative sequence: به + place (بیغوله‌ای) 'into a backwater/obscure village', شو (imperative of شدن) 'go/become', and فرود (verbal noun used as direction 'descend/settle down'). از مهان 'away/from the great/guests/court' indicates withdrawal from the high company — an instruction to retire from the public/noble sphere.
segments.s425-g46: که introduces a purpose or result clause 'so that'. کس نشنود combines the negative particle with the subjunctive/potential form نشنود ('may not hear' / 'let no one hear') — a jussive sense. نامت is 'your name' (نام + 2nd-ps suffix -ت). اندر جهان 'in the world' is the locative phrase indicating the domain in which nobody should hear the name.
segments.s425-g47: کزین بد 'from this bad/evil' is a prepositional phrase giving cause. ترا را is the older order of the 2nd-person pronominal object ('to you/your') where ت (enclitic) + را (object/topic marker) precede the possessed noun; تیره گردد روان is the verb phrase 'the soul becomes dark' (تیره گردد = 'will become bleak/dark', روان = 'soul/spirit'). The overall clause warns of a darkening of the soul as a consequence.
segments.s425-g48: بپرهیز is an imperative 'refrain/beware/avoid'. ازین is the poetic/older form of 'از این' meaning 'from this'. شهریار جوان is the noun phrase 'this young king' (شهریار = king; جوان = young) and is the object of avoidance.
segments.s425-g33: به دست + agent names the agent/agentive phrase in a passive construction: به here marks agency ('by'), دست is literal 'hand' used idiomatically for 'at the hands of', جوانی is the agent 'a young man', and چو + name is a simile 'like Esfandiyar' modifying جوانی. Word order (preposition + noun + simile) is typical of classical Persian poetic phrasing.
segments.s425-g34: This is the protasis (if-clause). اگر introduces the condition; تو is the 2nd-person pronoun; شوی is the present/subjunctive form of شدن 'become' used to form a passive (i.e. 'be (made)'); کشته is the past participle 'killed'; در کارزار is a locative prepositional phrase 'in battle'. Together: 'if you become killed in battle.'
segments.s425-g35: نماند is an impersonal/existential negative 'will not remain / there will be none left'. به زاولستان gives the location ('in/to Zabulistan') and آب و خاک are the things that will not remain ('water and soil'). The verb-front order is a common poetic inversion: verb + locative + objects.
segments.s425-g36: بلندی here denotes 'height/high places' (poetic noun) and is the subject; بر و بوم ('roofs and dwellings' / 'roof and homeland') specifies what is high; گردد is the verb 'become'; مغاک is the result 'an abyss'. So: 'the heights / roofs and homeland will become an abyss.'
segments.s425-g37: ور is a conditional 'if'; ایدونک is a small particle meaning roughly 'perhaps / by chance'; او را is the clitic-marked direct object 'to him' (or 'him' as experiencer); رسد here means 'befall / come to' (i.e. the harm occurs to him); زین گزند = 'this harm' (زین is an archaic/formal 'this' in the phrase, though that sense is not listed in the provided senses).
segments.s425-g38: نباشد is an impersonal negative 'there will not be' or 'may there not be'; ترا is the 2nd-person clitic marking possession/beneficiary ('for you'); را here functions as topic/emphasis after the clitic; نیز 'also' links this to what was just said; نام بلند 'a lofty name/reputation' is the phrase whose absence is asserted. Word order (verb before clitic + را) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s425-g39: همی is a frequentive/adverbial particle 'continually/ever'; هرکسی 'whoever / everyone' is the subject; داستانها 'tales/stories' is the object; زنند is the 3rd-person plural verb from زدن in the idiom داستان زدن 'to tell/recite tales' (here split as زن + ند = root + plural suffix).
segments.s425-g40: برآورده is a verbal/adjectival form meaning 'bringing forth / lifting up' (poetic use of برآوردن); نام ترا is 'your name' (ت is the 2nd-person suffix on نام), را is the object marker attached after the phrase, and بشکرند is 3rd-person plural 'they will thank/praise' (from شکریدن). The clause says people will bring your name forward and praise you.
segments.s425-g105: بدو here is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (classical order: indirect object before the verb). گفت is the third‑person past of گفتن 'he said' (the provided lemma senses don't include the verb form). زال‌ای پسر is a vocative phrase: the speaker (Zāl) is introduced and then addresses the youth as 'O son'; این سخن = 'these/this words/speech' is the direct object being referred to.
segments.s425-g106: مگوی is the negative imperative 'do not say/utter' (m‑prefix + گو). و is the simple conjunction 'and/then'. جدا کن is an imperative compound: جدا 'apart' + کن 'do' = 'separate/detach'. سرش را places the possessed object 'his head' (ش = enclitic 'his', را = definite object marker) after the verb, and ز بن uses poetic ز = 'from' + بن = 'base/trunk' meaning 'from the body/trunk'.
segments.s425-g107: که here introduces the subordinate clause with a purposive/lest sense ('that' / 'lest' / 'so that'). دیوانگان is plural 'madmen' (figuratively: uncontrolled people). این سخن 'these words' is the object, and بشنوند is the 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative of شنیدن 'may hear' (classical/poetic form expressing possibility or fear).
segments.s425-g108: بدین here functions as 'by/with this' (classical b- + ein = 'by this'); the dataset split is irregular, so دین as given doesn't match the usage (treated as part of بدین). خام گفتار = 'crude/naïve speech' (خام qualifying گفتار). تو is the possessive 'your', and نگروند is a 3rd‑person plural jussive/subjunctive meaning 'may adopt/accept' (from گرویدن in the sense 'to take on/adopt a belief or practice').
segments.s425-g109: قبادی is the proper name Qobād (here nominative/subject). به جایی نشسته is a prepositional phrase + perfect participle: 'has sat / sat down in a place' with جایی = 'somewhere' and نشسته = 'seated/sitting'; دژم is an adjective 'sullen/grim' describing his mood. Word order is Name + location phrase + participle + adjective (poetic ordering).
segments.s425-g110: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. تخت و کلاه lists symbols of rulership ('throne and crown/helmet') and گنج کهن is 'ancient/old treasure' (گنج modified by کهن). The sequence denies the presence of these royal signs and old wealth.
segments.s425-g111: چو functions as a simile 'like/as'. اسفندیاری is the name Esfandiyār. که is the relative 'who/that' introducing the relative clause. فعفور is an obscure/poetic/formal adjective here (the provided senses are uncertain), functioning as an intensifier for چین 'folds/wrinkles'—together describing him as marked by folds/wrinkles.
segments.s425-g112: نویسد همی is a classical/habitual present construction: نویسد 'writes' with the continuous/emphatic particle همی = 'still/continually', so 'is/continues to write' or 'is written'. نام او بر نگین is the object phrase 'his name on the gem/signet' with standard postverbal object order (نام او 'his name') and بر 'on/upon' + نگین 'gem/signet' marking the place.

sentences.s425-s61: Thus he spoke on until the sun rose over the mountain; his tongue did not grow weary of speaking.
sentences.s425-s9: Such a battlefield where two roaring lions enter into combat — both brave.
sentences.s425-s10: Now — what will Esfandiyar bring forth; what stratagem will he use in the moment of battle?
sentences.s425-s11: When tales of Rostam were heard, the old man's soul became full of anxious thought.
sentences.s425-s12: He said to him, “O famed champion, what did you say that has made my spirit dark?”
sentences.s425-s5: Zovāre commanded that whatever was said be brought, and that his treasure-house be produced from its hiding.
sentences.s425-s6: When Rostam saw the weapons of the fight, he thrust back his head and drew up a gust from his heart.
